terms.definition: Anything that takes up space and has weight. Everything around us, including air, water, and rocks, is made of matter.
terms.exampleUsage: Your desk, the water you drink, and the air you breathe are all made of matter.

terms.definition: A form of matter that has a fixed shape and a fixed size. Solids do not flow and you can hold them in your hand.
terms.exampleUsage: A wooden block is a solid because it keeps its shape no matter where you put it.

terms.definition: A form of matter that can flow and takes the shape of the container it is kept in. Liquids have a fixed volume but no fixed shape.
terms.exampleUsage: When you pour milk into a glass, it takes the shape of the glass because milk is a liquid.

terms.definition: A form of matter that has no fixed shape and no fixed size. Gases spread out to fill any space they are in.
terms.exampleUsage: The air inside a balloon is a gas that spreads out and fills the whole balloon.

terms.definition: The change of a solid into a liquid when it is heated. The solid gets warm and its particles start moving more freely.
terms.exampleUsage: An ice cream cone starts melting on a hot day and turns into a sticky liquid.

terms.definition: The change of a liquid into a solid when it is cooled down enough. The liquid becomes hard and gets a fixed shape.
terms.exampleUsage: When you put water in the freezer, it freezes and turns into ice cubes.

terms.definition: The change of a liquid into a gas. When a liquid gets heated, its particles move faster and escape into the air as gas.
terms.exampleUsage: After rain, the puddles on the ground dry up because the water evaporates into the air.

terms.definition: The change of a gas (water vapour) into a liquid when it cools down. Tiny water drops form on cool surfaces.
terms.exampleUsage: Cold water drops form on the outside of a chilled glass because water vapour in the air condenses on the cool surface.

terms.definition: Water in its gas form. Water vapour is invisible and is found in the air around us.
terms.exampleUsage: The steam that rises from a cup of hot tea is actually tiny water drops formed from water vapour.

terms.definition: The amount of space that something takes up. Solids and liquids have a fixed volume, but gases can expand or shrink.
terms.exampleUsage: A bottle holds a fixed volume of water, like 500 millilitres.

terms.definition: The very tiny pieces that make up all matter. Particles are so small that we cannot see them with our eyes.
terms.exampleUsage: Even a drop of water is made up of millions and millions of tiny particles.

terms.definition: When a liquid gets very hot and starts turning into gas with lots of bubbles. Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius.
terms.exampleUsage: When your mother boils water for tea, you can see bubbles forming and steam rising from the pot.
